innate.definition: Something you are born with; natural from birth.
innate.example: Babies have an innate ability to communicate.

capacity.definition: The ability to do or experience something.
capacity.example: We are born with a capacity for connection.

maternal.example: The maternal response experiment studied mothers and babies.

maintain.definition: To keep something going without stopping.
maintain.example: It was hard for mothers to maintain a still face.

powerful.example: A baby's signals are very powerful.

signals.definition: Actions or sounds that send a message.
signals.example: Babies use signals to communicate their needs.

interact.definition: To communicate and do things with another person.
interact.example: Babies want to interact from the moment they are born.

contraction.definition: When muscles tighten; during birth, the tightening of the womb to push the baby out.
contraction.example: A contraction is followed by a moment of rest.

produces.example: One person's action produces a response in the other.

rhythms.definition: Regular patterns of movement or activity.
rhythms.example: Parents and babies have their own different rhythms.

sibling.example: A baby and a sibling develop their own pattern.

co-created.definition: Made together by two or more people.
co-created.example: The relationship patterns are co-created by parent and baby.

fixed.example: These patterns are not fixed — they change.

input.definition: What someone puts in or contributes.
input.example: Each partner's input shapes the relationship.

readjustment.example: Some readjustment is needed when communication fails.

trial.definition: Testing something to see if it works.
trial.example: You learn by trial what your baby needs.

repairing.definition: Fixing something that was broken or went wrong.
repairing.example: Repairing past misses is part of good parenting.

glance.example: A certain glance may mean the baby wants more play.

interpret.definition: To understand the meaning of something.
interpret.example: Parents learn to interpret their baby's cries.

inadequate.example: She felt inadequate as a new mother.

thirsty.example: One cry might mean the baby is thirsty.

non-existent.example: There is no non-existent baby dictionary to help you.

dictionary.definition: A book that explains the meanings of words.
dictionary.example: Babies don't come with a dictionary.

established.definition: Set up and working; in place.
established.example: Once patterns are established, things get easier.

comics.definition: People who perform comedy to make audiences laugh.
comics.example: Good stand-up comics read the room.

stand-up.definition: A type of comedy where one person performs alone on stage.
stand-up.example: Stand-up comics adjust to their audience.

adapt.definition: To change to fit a new situation.
adapt.example: Good parents adapt to each child's needs.

audiences.definition: Groups of people who watch or listen to something.
audiences.example: No two audiences are the same.

performance.definition: The act of doing something in front of others.
performance.example: The comedian adjusted her performance for the crowd.

unconsciously.definition: Without being aware of it; automatically.
unconsciously.example: Most of this communication happens unconsciously.

communicating.definition: Sharing information or feelings with someone.
communicating.example: The baby was communicating from the very start.

glaze.definition: To become dull or unfocused, as if covered by a thin layer.
glaze.example: His eyes started to glaze over when he was tired.

tantrum.definition: A sudden outburst of anger, usually by a young child.
tantrum.example: If we don't notice the signs, a tantrum may follow.

spotted.example: I spotted that he needed a break.

nodded.definition: Moved the head up and down to say yes.
nodded.example: The child nodded when asked if he wanted to go outside.

daisies.definition: Small white flowers with yellow centers.
daisies.example: He started picking daisies in the grass.

familiar.definition: Well known because you have seen or experienced it before.
familiar.example: We began a familiar counting game.

spaced-out.definition: Not paying attention; looking dreamy or lost.
spaced-out.example: The child had a spaced-out look on his face.

absorbed.definition: Very interested in something; fully focused.
absorbed.example: The child was absorbed in playing with blocks.

triggers.definition: Things that cause a particular reaction.
triggers.example: He learned what his son's triggers were.

bodily.example: He learned to communicate on a bodily level.

manipulating.definition: Controlling someone or something in a clever but unfair way.
manipulating.example: Manipulating a baby into being quiet is harmful.

dehumanizing.definition: Treating someone as less than human.
dehumanizing.example: Ignoring a baby's needs is dehumanizing.

overadapt.definition: To change too much to please others, losing your true self.
overadapt.example: A child who must overadapt loses their sense of self.

humanity.definition: The quality of being human; kindness and compassion.
humanity.example: Every baby deserves to be treated with humanity.

practise.definition: To do something regularly to get better at it.
practise.example: If we practise observing, we understand children better.

restaurant.definition: A place where you pay to eat meals.
restaurant.example: We were at a restaurant with friends.

leaned.example: The child leaned against me for comfort.
